title: The Anger of Being Interrupted in Meetings
dek: *Anger in meetings isn't a flaw—it's a bodily alarm that registers disrespect. This isn't a guide to staying calm; it's an unflinching look at what your fury tells you, and how to stop swallowing it. You'll find no mantras here, only the raw truth of what the body remembers when your voice is stolen. Includes a practice, not a platitude.*
bodyMarkdown:
You have been told that your anger at being interrupted is a personal failing. The conference table hums under your forearms, and you have just drawn breath to speak. Three words in, Greg from marketing slices across your sentence like a wire through wet clay. Your lips part, then seal. The heat begins at your sternum, a small ember fanned by the silence you’ve just swallowed. Your throat closes, not with tears but with the sheer hydraulic pressure of an unuttered reply. Someone is saying “great point, Greg,” and your jaw clenches so fast you hear the grinding before you feel it. Your tongue sits thick, coated in a metallic film that tastes like the elevator button you pressed four times this morning, certain it would open for you. The anger that follows is not a shrill tantrum; it’s a full-body memo written in hot ink, a protest from the gut that knows you were just erased.

### The Mouth That Opens, Then Closes

Before you can even name the emotion, your body has named it for you. The trapezius muscles lock, pulling your shoulders toward your ears as if your skeleton is bracing for a blow. A cold sweat blooms on your palms, slicking the pen you’re supposed to be taking notes with. Your breath shrinks, reduced to shallow sips that never reach your lower ribs. This isn’t a choice; it’s a reflex older than your last performance review. The body remembers every time a voice has been snatched mid-syllable—the schoolteacher who praised the boy who shouted over you, the boyfriend who explained your own insight back to you, the father who talked through your dinner-table sentence. Each interruption layers onto your nervous system until a Tuesday morning project sync becomes the thousandth instance of your words being rendered optional.

The conference room’s neutral palette—beige walls, oatmeal carpet—suddenly feels hostile. You notice the exit sign, the hum of the HVAC, the way your colleague’s pen taps in a rhythm that syncopates with your rage. You are not “overreacting.” Your limbic system has just identified a threat to your social survival as precisely as it would a snarling dog. The anger is information, hot and urgent: you are being pushed out of the circle, and your body knows the cost of that exile.

### Your Anger Is a Memory, Not a Tantrum

Every time you’re cut off, you’re not merely annoyed at a conversational theft. You’re reliving the cumulative inventory of disregard. Soraya Chemaly reminds us that anger is an assertion of rights and worth—a cognitive flash that signals *I matter, and I will not be subtracted*. When you stifle it, you don’t just avoid a scene; you deposit another proof into your body that your substance is negotiable. The solar plexus tightens into a fist, the diaphragm becomes a drumhead stretched too tight, and somewhere behind your navel, a dull ache spreads like a bruise from the inside. This is the soma’s ledger. The body remembers every unexpressed protest, and it stores them in the fascia, the gut, the clenching molar.

The counterintuitive truth is that your fury is not a sign that you’re fragile. It’s proof that your self-preservation instinct still works. The real danger lies in the practiced calm you perform afterward—the seamless smile, the nod to Greg, the lighthearted “you took the words right out of my mouth” laugh that everyone in the room knows is a lie. That performance is a slow-acting poison, and your body knows it’s toxic long before your mind admits it.

### The Chemical Lie of Staying Calm

Professionalism demands that you ice over the anger. You’re supposed to breathe deeply, count to ten, and return to the agenda. But staying calm for the sake of office politics is a metabolic betrayal. When you suppress the physiological surge—the cortisol, the adrenaline, the drumbeat of your carotid—you don’t neutralize it. You simply redirect it inward. Your heart rate stays elevated well past the meeting’s end. Your blood vessels constrict. Your immune system takes a quiet hit. The body remembers the lie you asked it to swallow, and it sends the bill later, often as tension headaches, irritable bowel, or a mysterious weepiness at 9 p.m.

The wellness industry will sell you the idea that calmness is a choice, that you can lavender-oil your way out of systemic dismissal. But the anger is not a flaw in your self-regulation. It’s a correct reading of an unjust situation. When you’re told to “let it go,” you’re being asked to mistake gaslighting for maturity. The habit of eating your rage might keep the meeting moving, but it also carves a channel in your neural circuitry that teaches your brain: your voice does not matter, your body will comply. That channel deepens with every swallowed syllable.

### When They Patronize Your Tone

The moment you dare to push back, you’ll be told you sound “aggressive” or “defensive.” The interrupter, still smug, leans back and says, “I didn’t mean to upset you—let’s keep this constructive.” A colleague offers, “Maybe let’s take the emotion out of it.” The table returns to him. Your anger, now visible, gets twisted into a liability. They’ll use your tone as a cudgel, as if the heat in your voice invalidates the idea you were trying to express.

But Thomas Moore, in his study of the soul’s darker textures, insists that anger is a necessary visitor, not an intruder to be shamed away. It is a symptom of the soul’s neglected landscape, and it arrives, uninvited, to show you where your deep caring lives. If you banish it, you banish the part of you that knows you deserve a full sentence. To call your anger “unprofessional” is a political maneuver, not a human truth. It’s a way of turning a power play into a personality flaw—yours.

### Thich Nhat Hanh’s Bullet Train, Thomas Moore’s Soul

Thich Nhat Hanh once wrote that anger is a storm rising from the bottom of your consciousness, and that when it comes, you must not act immediately. You must breathe, cradle the emotion like a crying infant, and wait for the winds to quiet. This is wise in a monastery. In a conference room, that pause is exactly what the steamroller counts on. If you retreat into your breath for fifteen seconds, the moment to speak is gone; your point has been co-opted, the agenda moved on, and you are left with only a calm nervous system and a silent mouth. The practice of stopping to breathe can easily become another tool of self-erasure when the context demands your immediate voice.

So what do we do with the storm? Moore would counsel that we don’t simply calm it; we listen to it. The anger holds specific intelligence about what you value. It carries the outline of your unspoken contribution, the phantom shape of the idea that was intercepted. Instead of trying to vanish the heat, you can learn to hold it without letting it scald. Later, when you’re alone, you can replay the interruption not to ruminate but to extract its message: *This is what I care about. This is the boundary they crossed.* You don’t need a Zen cushion; you need a few square inches of silence in your car or a bathroom stall where you can let the shakes come without apologizing for them.

### A Five-Minute Practice for the Clenched Jaw

You’re out of the room now, but your body is still braced as if the next blow is coming. Find a private space—a single-occupancy restroom, a stairwell, your parked car. Sit with your feet flat and place both hands on your jaw. Feel the heat radiating from your masseter muscles, which have been grinding words into powder. First, intentionally clench your jaw harder, as hard as you can, for five seconds. Let the pressure build until your molars ache, then release with a long, audible exhale. Do this twice more. When you release, notice the tremble in your lips, the flood of warmth that follows the tension.

Next, rub a drop of [doTERRA Lavender Essential Oil (15 mL)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00F23LHJA?tag=spankyspinola-20) between your palms and cup them over your nose. The scent won’t dissolve the memory of the slight, but it tells your limbic brain that the immediate threat is over. Then, with your eyes open, name out loud five objects you see in the space. The soap dispenser. The tile grout. The light switch. This isn’t a gratitude list; it’s a reality anchor that keeps you from spinning into the abstract fury of why they always do this. Finally, place one hand on your sternum and press firmly, breathing into that pressure. Remind yourself: the anger is complete as it is. It does not need to become a polite email or a meditative mantra to be valid.

If the cycle of interruption is chronic—if your jaw is permanently sore and your sleep shredded by 3 a.m. replays—herbal support can be a practical ally, not a spiritual bypass. A cup of [Yogi Kava Stress Relief Tea (16 tea bags)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00I7JHC2S?tag=spankyspinola-20) after work signals your body to downshift from the day’s cortisol spike. For deeper, systemic depletion that shows up as chronic irritability, a formula like [Plum Flower An Shen Bu Xin Wan (200 pills)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B007WK0E56?tag=spankyspinola-20), which nourishes the heart and calms the spirit in traditional practice, may be a quiet tool to restore your nervous system’s baseline. These are not cures; they are bandages for a wound that deserves stopping at its source.

### Questions You Ask When No One’s Listening

**“Isn’t it better to just let it go and pick your battles?”**  
The question assumes anger is a pathology you must triage. But the body doesn’t “let go” on command; it stores. You can decide not to fight this particular battle, but you still need to discharge the physiological residue. Otherwise, you’re just shoving the anger into your tissues, where it will curdle into resentment, chronic pain, or the kind of exhaustion that makes you feel hollow by Wednesday. Picking battles is strategic; feeling the anger fully is non-negotiable.

**“How do I speak up without being labeled aggressive?”**  
You may not be able to control their label, but you can reroute the conversation. When interrupted, try a neutral but unwavering phrase like, “I wasn’t finished,” delivered without a smile. Do not soften it with “sorry, just one more thing.” Let the silence after your statement belong to you. If they accuse you of aggression, you can say, “I am speaking at the same volume I had before. My sentence was incomplete.” This won’t fix the culture, but it refuses the role of the agreeable victim. Your body will still shake afterward—let it.

**“What if my anger scares me?”**  
Anger often scares people who’ve been taught that their rage is monstrous. That fear is a muzzle installed by a world that benefits from your silence. But your anger isn’t a threat to anyone else; it’s a set of sensations in your own body—a quickened pulse, a hot face, a knot in the belly. Sitting with those sensations without acting on them teaches you that you can contain your own power. You are not a bomb; you’re a person with a legitimate grievance. The fear isn’t a stop sign; it’s a cue to get curious about what you’ve been told about your own force.

### The Room Full of Ghosts

The meeting ends, and the room empties. You gather your notebook, your half-empty water glass, your phone slick with fingerprints. The anger does not leave with the others; it stays, a low hum under your skin, a frequency that moves through your bloodstream like a rumor. You are not alone in this hum. There are other versions of you sitting at this same table, or tables just like it, in buildings across the city, all walking out with the same metallic taste, the same tight jaw, the same cellular memory of being made small.

Your anger is not a private neurosis. It is a ghost light illuminating the countless voices that have been cut off in rooms like this for decades. When you refuse to pathologize it, when you let it teach you where your boundaries lie, you stop performing calm for people who depend on your silence. You might not fix the meeting culture tomorrow, but you will walk out of this building with your body still your own, the anger no longer an enemy but a difficult, vital companion. And the next time your mouth opens in that room, you will know, even before you speak, that the heat rising in your chest is not a flaw. It is a compass, ancient and unapologetic, pointing toward what you refuse to lose again. For a deeper look at how the body’s signals become a language you can trust, you might explore [this companion piece](https://theoraclelover.com).
